lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — Potelligent — Kyowa's FUT8 knockout
So the Kyowa Hakko team had shown, by 2003, that afucosylated IgG was a much better ADCC drug.
Now they needed to industrialize it.
They needed a production system — a cell line — that would make afucosylated antibody, reliably, in large quantities, at the quality demanded for human therapeutics.
They needed Potelligent.
Here's the engineering problem.
The standard host cell for producing therapeutic antibodies is the Chinese hamster ovary cell, CHO.
CHO has been the workhorse of biopharma for forty years.
It folds antibodies well, secretes them at high titer, grows in suspension culture, scales to ten-thousand-liter bioreactors, has been characterized to death by every regulator on Earth.
If you want to make an FDA-approved antibody, you make it in CHO.
But CHO cells have a complete fucosylation machinery.
They express FUT8, the fucosyltransferase enzyme that adds core fucose to N-glycans.
They have GMD and FX, the enzymes that synthesize the GDP-fucose substrate FUT8 uses.
They have GFR, the transporter that delivers GDP-fucose into the Golgi apparatus where the glycan decoration happens.
The CHO cell, by default, makes antibodies that are about 95% core-fucosylated.

To make afucosylated antibody at scale, you need to disable this machinery — but ideally without disabling anything else, without slowing the cell down, without compromising titer, without changing any other glycan structures.
The Kyowa Hakko team's solution was elegant: knock out FUT8 itself.
The single enzyme that does the core fucosylation, gone.
If FUT8 is absent, there's no core fucose added, no matter how much GDP-fucose is sitting around in the Golgi.
And because FUT8 has only one job in the cell — adding core fucose to N-glycans on glycoproteins — knocking it out doesn't break anything else.
The cell still grows.
It still makes antibody.
It still secretes the antibody.
It just makes antibody without core fucose.
This was the Potelligent technology, branded by Kyowa Hakko Kirin (later BioWa) and licensed widely across the industry.

The first FUT8 knockout was made by classical gene targeting in CHO cells — a slow, painstaking process of homologous recombination, identifying clones where both alleles of the FUT8 gene had been disrupted, screening for ones that grew well and made good antibody.
The original knockout line was published by Yamane-Ohnuki and colleagues in 2004, and it became the platform for an entire generation of glycoengineered drugs.

The remarkable thing about FUT8-knockout CHO is that the cells are essentially indistinguishable from wild-type CHO in growth, titer, and product quality — except that every antibody molecule they secrete is afucosylated.
You don't have to do anything clever.
You just put your antibody gene into the cell line, grow it up, harvest the antibody.
The drug comes out afucosylated by default.
That's why Potelligent is sometimes called a "drop-in" technology — you swap the cell line, you get the glycoform you want, and everything else about the development process stays the same.
There's one important nuance to mention.
FUT8 is the gene, but the function is core alpha-1,6 fucosylation — that's the chemical linkage.
FUT8 attaches fucose to the GlcNAc with an alpha-1,6 linkage, which is the linkage on the core fucose specifically.
There are other fucosyltransferases in cells that make other types of fucose linkages — alpha-1,3, alpha-1,2 — but those don't decorate the antibody N-glycan core.
So knocking out FUT8 is sufficient and specific.
You don't have to knock out the whole fucose biosynthesis pathway; you just have to remove the one enzyme that does the antibody-relevant decoration.
Today, FUT8 knockouts can be made much more efficiently than the original homologous-recombination approach.
CRISPR-Cas9 has made it routine to generate FUT8-knockout CHO lines in months rather than years.
Several companies maintain their own internal FUT8-knockout platforms now — they don't need to license Potelligent.
But Potelligent was the first, and it remains the benchmark against which other afucose platforms are measured.

The first major drug made on Potelligent was mogamulizumab — we'll cover it in detail in Segment 8 — and the second was obinutuzumab, which used a related but distinct glycoengineering approach licensed from another company.
But the foundation, the platform, the proof that you could industrially produce a fully afucosylated antibody at therapeutic quality — that was Potelligent.
And it cracked open a whole therapeutic class.
